paper_title: Interatomic Coulombic decay initiated by electron removal and excitation processes in helium ion and argon dimer collisions

perturbations.why_wrong: The text later describes rare gas dimers as 'loosely bonded' and vulnerable to ionization, which contradicts the claim that they are formed by 'strong covalent interactions'. Rare gas dimers are held by weak van der Waals forces.

perturbations.original: In most of the experimental works discussed in the review paper~\cite{Jahnke20}, photo excitation was used to initiate ICD in the target systems. An alternative approach are ion-cluster collisions, that are particularly relevant pathways for producing showers of low-energy electrons through ICD that damage DNA~\cite{Bouda2000,Alizadeh_2015} with leftover radical species that can react with biomolecules in living tissue~\cite{Vendrell_2010,Pettersson_2013}.
perturbations.perturbed: In most of the experimental works discussed in the review paper~\cite{Jahnke20}, photo excitation was used to initiate ICD in the target systems. An alternative approach are ion-cluster collisions, that are particularly relevant pathways for producing showers of high-energy electrons through ICD that damage DNA~\cite{Bouda2000,Alizadeh_2015} with leftover radical species that can react with biomolecules in living tissue~\cite{Vendrell_2010,Pettersson_2013}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims ICD produces 'high-energy electrons', but the abstract and subsequent sections (e.g., S0003) discuss low kinetic energy release (KER) and the specific energy transfer mechanism of ICD which typically results in low-energy electrons.

perturbations.original: ICD can be experimentally detected from the kinetic energy release spectra of the fragmented ions, ideally recorded in coincidence with the ICD electron. Observing ICD is not easy given multiple other competing fragmentation mechanisms such as radiative charge transfer (RCT) and Coulomb explosion (CE)~\cite{Jahnke20}, resulting in similar signals. RCT involves the removal of two valence electrons from one of the two atoms, followed by radiative relaxation to a Ar$^{+}(3p^{-1})$+Ar$^{+}(3p^{-1})$ state. CE in its simplest case is a process in which two singly charged ions are formed from electron removal from each atom, followed by fragmentation. A reason as to why ICD was first experimentally detected in neon clusters and dimers is because of the simple pathway for ICD in these systems along with the clean resulting spectral signal~\cite{Jahnke04}. 2$s$ electron removal from one neon atom provides an energetically sufficient pathway for ICD which has been observed in several experiments, including in ion-dimer collisions~\cite{Kim13}. Although it is a common and simple test-bed for ICD, it is worth exploring different systems in which this decay originates in more intricate and less clear ways, such as in an argon dimer.
perturbations.perturbed: ICD can be experimentally detected from the kinetic energy release spectra of the fragmented ions, ideally recorded in coincidence with the ICD electron. Observing ICD is not easy given multiple other competing fragmentation mechanisms such as radiative charge transfer (RCT) and Coulomb explosion (CE)~\cite{Jahnke20}, resulting in similar signals. RCT involves the removal of two valence electrons from one of the two atoms, followed by radiative relaxation to a Ar$^{+}(3p^{-1})$+Ar$^{+}(3p^{-1})$ state. CE in its simplest case is a process in which two singly charged ions are formed from electron removal from each atom, followed by fragmentation. A reason as to why ICD was first experimentally detected in neon clusters and dimers is because of the complex pathway for ICD in these systems along with the noisy resulting spectral signal~\cite{Jahnke04}. 2$s$ electron removal from one neon atom provides an energetically sufficient pathway for ICD which has been observed in several experiments, including in ion-dimer collisions~\cite{Kim13}. Although it is a common and simple test-bed for ICD, it is worth exploring different systems in which this decay originates in more intricate and less clear ways, such as in an argon dimer.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims neon was the first detected due to a 'complex pathway' and 'noisy signal', but the very next sentence describes it as a 'common and simple test-bed' and the original text notes it was chosen for its 'clean resulting spectral signal'.

perturbations.original: Although Ar is also a noble gas with a filled outer electron shell, removal of an inner valence 3$s$ electron provides insufficient relaxation energy to ionize a neighbouring argon atom's valence electron. An energy of 19.6 eV is needed to remove an electron from the neighbouring argon atom, where 15.8 eV is the ionization energy and 3.8 eV is the minimum shared kinetic energy release (KER) between the argon ion fragments and the ICD electron~\cite{Zhang_2025}. The only relaxation pathways that have the requisite energy are those from the Ar$^{+}(3p^{-2}nl)$ configurations, starting with Ar$^{+}(3p^{-2}3d)$ for which the majority of LS coupled states are energetically open~\cite{Kimura_2013}.
perturbations.perturbed: Although Ar is also a noble gas with a filled outer electron shell, removal of an inner valence 3$s$ electron provides sufficient relaxation energy to ionize a neighbouring argon atom's valence electron. An energy of 19.6 eV is needed to remove an electron from the neighbouring argon atom, where 15.8 eV is the ionization energy and 3.8 eV is the minimum shared kinetic energy release (KER) between the argon ion fragments and the ICD electron~\cite{Zhang_2025}. The only relaxation pathways that have the requisite energy are those from the Ar$^{+}(3p^{-2}nl)$ configurations, starting with Ar$^{+}(3p^{-2}3d)$ for which the majority of LS coupled states are energetically open~\cite{Kimura_2013}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text states that 3s removal provides 'sufficient' energy, but then immediately states that 19.6 eV is needed and that 'the only relaxation pathways that have the requisite energy are those from the Ar+(3p^-2 nl) configurations', implying 3s is insufficient.

perturbations.original: It was noted in Kim \textit{et al.}~\cite{Kim13} that at impact energies of 150 keV/amu of He$^{2+}$ collisions with the argon dimer target, the KER and electron energy ICD spectral signal was less defined than in the case of the neon dimer target. This is due to a more complex electron configuration requirement for ICD generation in the collision system. In this work we investigate the electron dynamics preceding argon dimer fragmentation by He$^{2+}$ and He$^{+}$ impact at energies ranging from 10 keV/amu to 150 keV/amu. This range of energies offers an additional opportunity to examine the transitions in dominant electron-removal pathways from capture to ionization from low to high impact energies. We present a study on the electron dynamics in these collision systems to shed light on the ICD signature for the argon dimer target.
perturbations.perturbed: It was noted in Kim \textit{et al.}~\cite{Kim13} that at impact energies of 150 keV/amu of He$^{2+}$ collisions with the argon dimer target, the KER and electron energy ICD spectral signal was more defined than in the case of the neon dimer target. This is due to a more complex electron configuration requirement for ICD generation in the collision system. In this work we investigate the electron dynamics preceding argon dimer fragmentation by He$^{2+}$ and He$^{+}$ impact at energies ranging from 10 keV/amu to 150 keV/amu. This range of energies offers an additional opportunity to examine the transitions in dominant electron-removal pathways from capture to ionization from low to high impact energies. We present a study on the electron dynamics in these collision systems to shed light on the ICD signature for the argon dimer target.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims the argon signal was 'more defined' than neon, but the following sentence explains this is due to a 'more complex electron configuration requirement', which logically suggests a less defined or more difficult to observe signal.

The text defines the Independent Electron Model (IEM) but then claims 'electron-electron correlations are fully included', which contradicts the fundamental definition of an independent electron model.

perturbations.original: The static potential considered so far has been referred to as the no response model. It is deemed accurate when the projectile impact velocity is large compared to the average orbital velocity $\bar{v}$ of an outer target electron. For the Ar valence electrons, $\bar{v}$ corresponds to an impact energy of approximately 30 keV/amu. At such impact velocities, the electron density of the target potential does not substantially change over the course of the brief collision. However, at low to intermediate projectile impact energies, time-dependent screening effects play an important role. Factoring this in, a non-zero time-dependent potential is used in what is called the response model. To account for target response during ionization and capture throughout the collision in a global fashion, the approach presented in~\cite{tom00} is used. In it the dynamic target potential is described by a linear combination of ionic ground-state potentials, weighted by time-dependent probabilities to produce a fractional charge during the collision~\cite{tom00}. The idea behind the response model is to adjust the atomic potential in the spirit of time-dependent mean-field theory to reflect the fractional ionic character a target atom acquires when electrons are removed from it. The ionic character is regulated by the time-dependent net electron removal probability. The response effects remain marginal so long as zero- and one-fold electron removal dominate, but they may become significant once higher-order removal processes contribute substantially. The projectile impact energy dependence of the response model will be later discussed in section \ref{sec:Results}.
perturbations.perturbed: The static potential considered so far has been referred to as the no response model. It is deemed accurate when the projectile impact velocity is small compared to the average orbital velocity $\bar{v}$ of an outer target electron. For the Ar valence electrons, $\bar{v}$ corresponds to an impact energy of approximately 30 keV/amu. At such impact velocities, the electron density of the target potential does not substantially change over the course of the brief collision. However, at low to intermediate projectile impact energies, time-dependent screening effects play an important role. Factoring this in, a non-zero time-dependent potential is used in what is called the response model. To account for target response during ionization and capture throughout the collision in a global fashion, the approach presented in~\cite{tom00} is used. In it the dynamic target potential is described by a linear combination of ionic ground-state potentials, weighted by time-dependent probabilities to produce a fractional charge during the collision~\cite{tom00}. The idea behind the response model is to adjust the atomic potential in the spirit of time-dependent mean-field theory to reflect the fractional ionic character a target atom acquires when electrons are removed from it. The ionic character is regulated by the time-dependent net electron removal probability. The response effects remain marginal so long as zero- and one-fold electron removal dominate, but they may become significant once higher-order removal processes contribute substantially. The projectile impact energy dependence of the response model will be later discussed in section \ref{sec:Results}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text says the 'no response model' is accurate when velocity is 'small', but then explains that at such velocities the density 'does not substantially change over the course of the brief collision', which describes a high-velocity (fast) collision.

perturbations.original: For these potentials, basis states need to be constructed in accordance with the assumptions made so far in order to represent the time-dependent Schr{\"o}dinger equation that describes the ion-atom collision system~\cite{tcbgm} and to extract transition amplitudes to be used in the analysis and submodels presented in \ref{sec:model}. This is done using the two-center basis generator method (TC-BGM) where both target and projectile centres are used for the construction of basis states. The promising aspect of this method is that the model space spanned by the basis functions is dynamically adapted to the time-dependent problem, thereby minimizing the couplings to the Hilbert space not covered by the basis states~\cite{OJKroneisen_1999}.
perturbations.perturbed: For these potentials, basis states need to be constructed in accordance with the assumptions made so far in order to represent the time-dependent Schr{\"o}dinger equation that describes the ion-atom collision system~\cite{tcbgm} and to extract transition amplitudes to be used in the analysis and submodels presented in \ref{sec:model}. This is done using the two-center basis generator method (TC-BGM) where only target centres are used for the construction of basis states. The promising aspect of this method is that the model space spanned by the basis functions is dynamically adapted to the time-dependent problem, thereby minimizing the couplings to the Hilbert space not covered by the basis states~\cite{OJKroneisen_1999}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text identifies the method as the 'two-center basis generator method' but then claims 'only target centres' are used, which contradicts the 'two-center' name and the subsequent mention of projectile orbitals in S0008.

perturbations.original: In the present work, the basis states for argon consist of the $2s$ to $4f$ target orbitals as well as the $1s$ to $4f$ projectile orbitals and a set of pseudo-states~\cite{Kirchner_2021}. The argon $1s$ orbital is not included given the far higher energy requirement to remove a K-shell electron in comparison to those in the L and M shells. In the case of He$^{+}$ impact, there are $N=N_{T}+1$ bound electrons; $N_{T}$ and 1 on the target and projectile respectively. Transition amplitudes for electron changing states are obtained from solving the $N$ single-electron time-dependent Schrödinger equations corresponding to the single-particle Hamiltonian (\ref{eq:hamiltonian}).
perturbations.perturbed: In the present work, the basis states for argon consist of the $2s$ to $4f$ target orbitals as well as the $1s$ to $4f$ projectile orbitals and a set of pseudo-states~\cite{Kirchner_2021}. The argon $1s$ orbital is included given the low energy requirement to remove a K-shell electron in comparison to those in the L and M shells. In the case of He$^{+}$ impact, there are $N=N_{T}+1$ bound electrons; $N_{T}$ and 1 on the target and projectile respectively. Transition amplitudes for electron changing states are obtained from solving the $N$ single-electron time-dependent Schrödinger equations corresponding to the single-particle Hamiltonian (\ref{eq:hamiltonian}).
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims the Ar 1s orbital is included due to 'low energy requirement', but K-shell (1s) electrons are known to have much higher binding energies than L (2s, 2p) and M (3s, 3p) shells, as implied by the original text's exclusion of it.

perturbations.original: The collision system is set up with the argon dimer fixed at a bond length of $R_e=3.76$ a.u.~\cite{Moseley_1977}, while the projectile travels parallel to the dimer axis at impact parameters $b$ ranging from 0.2 to 10 a.u.. Electron dynamics are studied from the sequential ion-atom collisions between the projectile and the argon atoms. Electron removal in coincidence with excitation is of particular interest as a method of facilitating ICD in the argon dimer. Not all electron excitation configurations are energetically able to facilitate ICD in an argon dimer, and a scaling method to account for this is described later in section \ref{sec:model}. At low impact energies, electron removal is practically equivalent with electron capture by the projectile, but this is not the case at projectile speeds above $\bar{v}$. The transition in dominance between capture and ionization processes is accounted for and described in section \ref{sec:model} of this paper.
perturbations.perturbed: The collision system is set up with the argon dimer fixed at a bond length of $R_e=3.76$ a.u.~\cite{Moseley_1977}, while the projectile travels parallel to the dimer axis at impact parameters $b$ ranging from 0.2 to 10 a.u.. Electron dynamics are studied from the sequential ion-atom collisions between the projectile and the argon atoms. Electron removal in coincidence with excitation is of particular interest as a method of facilitating ICD in the argon dimer. All electron excitation configurations are energetically able to facilitate ICD in an argon dimer, and a scaling method to account for this is described later in section \ref{sec:model}. At low impact energies, electron removal is practically equivalent with electron capture by the projectile, but this is not the case at projectile speeds above $\bar{v}$. The transition in dominance between capture and ionization processes is accounted for and described in section \ref{sec:model} of this paper.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims 'All electron excitation configurations' can facilitate ICD, but S0003 explicitly states that 3s removal provides 'insufficient relaxation energy' and only specific configurations like 3p^-2 nl have the requisite energy.

The text contradicts itself by stating the multinomial analysis is preferred, while the section title and subsequent paragraphs explicitly describe and utilize the determinantal analysis as the primary model.

perturbations.original: The determinantal analysis is a statistical method in which determinants of single-particle density matrices are computed and the Pauli exclusion principle is accounted for. The electron removal processes from the dimer include the following categories: one electron removal, one electron removal from each atom in the dimer (two-site), and two electron removal from a single atom (one-site), one electron removal and one electron excitation on a single atom. The previously developed fixed-charge and capture models I and II~\cite{Starko_2025} are applied in order to examine the repercussions on electron dynamics in the collision system depending on projectile charge.
perturbations.perturbed: The determinantal analysis is a statistical method in which determinants of single-particle density matrices are computed and the Pauli exclusion principle is accounted for. The electron removal processes from the dimer include the following categories: one electron removal, one electron removal from each atom in the dimer (two-site), and two electron removal from a single atom (one-site), one electron removal and one electron excitation on a single atom. The previously developed fixed-charge and capture models I and II~\cite{Starko_2025} are applied in order to examine the repercussions on electron dynamics in the collision system, assuming the projectile charge remains constant throughout the interaction.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text states the models examine dynamics depending on projectile charge but then claims they assume the charge remains constant, which contradicts the description of 'capture models' where electron capture necessarily changes the projectile charge.

perturbations.original: The determinantal analysis and its submodels are further subdivided by considering them with a frozen target potential (no-response) model and a dynamic response model following the details laid out in~\cite{tom00} and applied in~\cite{Starko_2025}.
perturbations.perturbed: The determinantal analysis and its submodels are further subdivided by considering them with a frozen target potential (no-response) model and a dynamic response model, where both models assume the target potential remains static during the entire collision process.
perturbations.why_wrong: The definition of a 'dynamic response model' is inherently contradictory to the claim that it assumes the target potential remains static.

perturbations.original: The dynamical response approach for each analysis and model is conducted as the no-response model, except that the final-state analyses are carried out at eleven, instead of just one, final distances between the projectile and the target, with results averaged. Due to the dynamically changing potential, projecting the solutions onto inital target eigenstates results in fluctuating transition probabilities~\cite{tom00}. This then requires calculating these probabilities by sampling a set of distances (ranging from $z=30$ a.u. to $50$ a.u. in the present work) and then averaging~\cite{Starko_2025}.
perturbations.perturbed: The dynamical response approach for each analysis and model is conducted as the no-response model, except that the final-state analyses are carried out at eleven, instead of just one, final distances between the projectile and the target, with results averaged. Due to the dynamically changing potential, projecting the solutions onto inital target eigenstates results in transition probabilities that are independent of the distance at which the projection occurs. This then requires calculating these probabilities by sampling a set of distances (ranging from $z=30$ a.u. to $50$ a.u. in the present work) and then averaging~\cite{Starko_2025}.
perturbations.why_wrong: If transition probabilities were independent of distance, there would be no logical need to sample a set of distances and average them as described in the next sentence.

perturbations.original: For the He$^{+}$ projectile, capture of an electron results in a neutral atom for which we assume that its interaction with a subsequent atom cannot trigger electron removal, i.e. we set the elastic probability equal to one in this scenario. This means He$^{+}$ is limited to single electron capture.
perturbations.perturbed: For the He$^{+}$ projectile, capture of an electron results in a neutral atom for which we assume that its interaction with a subsequent atom cannot trigger electron removal, i.e. we set the elastic probability equal to zero in this scenario. This means He$^{+}$ is limited to single electron capture.
perturbations.why_wrong: Setting the elastic probability to zero implies a 100% chance of an inelastic event (electron removal), which contradicts the statement that the neutral atom cannot trigger electron removal.
In atomic collisions, direct ionization typically increases in importance with impact energy; the model's purpose is to account for this growth, not a decrease.

perturbations.perturbed: Transition amplitudes are assumed to be described by inner products of Slater determinants, that by design account for the Pauli exclusion principle~\cite{Starko_2025}. The transition from initial-to-final-state electron configurations results in a probability that is equal to the determinant of a single-particle density matrix. Here $\left|i_{j},t_{f}\right\rangle$ is the time-propagated orbital corresponding to the initial state $\left|i_{j}\right\rangle$, $\left|f_{j}\right\rangle$ is a final state, $\gamma_{jk}$ are the density matrix elements computed from the Slater determinants, and $N_{T}$ is the number of active target electrons ($N_{T}$=2 in the present work). The density matrix elements are sums of products of single-particle transition amplitudes from a range of initial to final states.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text identifies the target as an argon dimer (Ar2). Since a single Ar atom has 8 valence electrons, $N_T$ must be at least 16 for a dimer, making $N_T=2$ factually inconsistent with the system description.

perturbations.perturbed: When we explicitly specify the final states of all electrons in the system, as in equation (\ref{eq:exclusive}), the resulting quantity is referred to as an inclusive probability. This represents a fully resolved, complete measurement. In practice, however, such detailed information is rarely accessible. More commonly, we consider the probability for certain electron transitions without fixing the final states of all remaining electrons. This is known as an exclusive probability~\cite{Ludde_1985}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The definitions of inclusive and exclusive probabilities are swapped. An exclusive probability specifies all states (fully resolved), while an inclusive probability leaves some unspecified.

perturbations.original:
\subsubsection{Removal without Electron Excitation}
  
The inclusive model can be extended to include the projectile ion in the analysis. For He$^{2+}$ impact, we consider two models that take into account the capture of an electron by the projectile to form a He$^{+}$ ion and its interaction with the second atom in the dimer; models I and II~\cite{Starko_2025}.
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsubsection{Removal without Electron Excitation}
  
The inclusive model can be extended to include the projectile ion in the analysis. For He$^{2+}$ impact, we consider two models that ignore the capture of an electron by the projectile, assuming it remains a He$^{2+}$ ion during its interaction with the second atom in the dimer; models I and II~\cite{Starko_2025}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text describes 'capture models' (Models I and II) but then claims they ignore electron capture, which is a direct contradiction of the model names and purpose.

perturbations.original: Model I incorporates the above inclusive probability formulation, yet using transition amplitudes computed from interactions between He$^{+}$ and an Ar atom in addition to those for He$^{2+}-$Ar collisions. The channel probabilities are computed in an analogous manner as in the fixed-charge model approach.
perturbations.perturbed: Model I incorporates the above inclusive probability formulation, yet using transition amplitudes computed from interactions between He$^{+}$ and an Ar atom in addition to those for He$^{2+}-$Ar collisions. The channel probabilities are computed using a multinomial distribution rather than the determinantal approach used in the fixed-charge model.
perturbations.why_wrong: Section 2.1 (S0010) explicitly states that the multinomial analysis is not examined in this work in favor of the determinantal approach.
